⭐ ✨ 🌟
Educator Resource · Part B · EDF5828

Teaching with
Going Fishing with Nana

A multimodal resource for Foundation educators exploring oral language and early literacy learning through shared picture book reading 📚

AuthorsFrances & Lindsay Haji-Ali
IllustratorKaren Briggs
PublisherMagabala Books, 2025
CBCA2026 Notable – Early Childhood
LevelTowards Foundation / Foundation
About the Book

A journey through Country, language & connection 🦘

Going Fishing with Nana follows a young girl and her grandmother on a fishing trip through the Kimberley region of northwestern Australia. As they drive through the red earth landscape, they encounter a series of native animals — each watching them in a different way.

A repeated refrain runs throughout the book, inviting children to predict what comes next. The story closes warmly at sunset, and the illustrated "Mud Map to Nana's" traces their real-world journey across Western Australia and the Northern Territory.

🎣

"We're going fishing, Nana and me.
We're going fishing – what will we see?"

CBCA Status
2026 Notable – Early Childhood
Recommended Age
2+ years
Key Features
Repetition, rhyme, counting, adjectives
Cultural Context
Yawuru/Karajarri & Yorta Yorta

🐸 Frogs Peeping

🦘 Kangaroos Peeking
Three Classroom Activities

Language & Literacy Activities 🎉

Each activity draws directly from the linguistic features of the book to support oral language development and early literacy learning in Foundation settings.

🎵
Activity 1 · Phonological Awareness

Shared Reading & Prediction

Overview

The teacher reads the book aloud and pauses before the end of each refrain, inviting children to complete the line. On the second reading, children predict what animal might appear before the page is turned.

"We're going fishing – what will we ___?" 🤔

Steps
  1. Read the book aloud once through so children absorb the rhythm and pattern.
  2. On the second reading, pause at the end of each refrain and gesture to children to join in.
  3. Before turning each page, ask "What do you think we will see next?"
  4. Celebrate all responses and connect children's predictions to the illustration.
Materials
  • A copy of Going Fishing with Nana
  • Optional: printed refrain strips for children to hold
Curriculum Links

📋 VEYLDF Learning Outcome 5

Children as effective communicators who interact with texts and express meaning through verbal and non-verbal responses. Familiarity with language patterns supports children's movement from passive listening to active participation.

Phonological Awareness Oral Language Text Prediction
Diverse Learners 🌏

The predictable and repeated structure lowers the language barrier for EAL/D students, allowing them to participate through rhythm and pattern before they can produce the full sentence independently. For children who need additional challenge, they can be invited to substitute their own animal and create a new line. First Nations students may find the cultural setting and characters affirming and engaging.

📚 Academic Justification

Read and Quirke (2018) demonstrated that rhyme and word placement in storybooks actively support young children's ability to map and retain new words, in part because the rhythmic structure creates natural pauses that prompt children to anticipate upcoming vocabulary. This active prediction is significant: when children supply a missing word, they engage in the kind of active processing that supports deeper learning. Ziolkowski and Goldstein (2008) further found that embedding phonological awareness opportunities within repeated book reading produced meaningful gains in language skills for preschool children, suggesting that returning to this text across multiple readings will continue to strengthen children's phonological awareness over time.

If the video below does not load, click here to watch: https://youtu.be/dSh9RnEgzGg

Shared Reading & Prediction
👁️
Activity 2 · Vocabulary Depth

Ways of Looking

Overview

Using the nine "looking" verbs from the book, children act out each word using facial expressions and body language, then discuss how each word feels different. They then play a paired guessing game to practise the verbs with each other.

watchingpeepingglancing squintingglaringpeeking gazinglookingstaring
Steps
  1. After reading the book, revisit each animal page and draw attention to the "looking" verb.
  2. Say the word together: "How does that feel? Can you show me with your face and body?"
  3. Children act out each verb as a whole group — squinting eyes, widening for a gaze, frowning for a glare.
  4. Children then do "You Do, I Guess" game in pairs: one child acts out a "looking" verb while the other guesses which verb it is, then they swap roles.
  5. Bring the class back together: "Which was the trickiest verb to act out? Which was easiest to guess?"
🎮 You Do, I Guess!

In pairs: one child acts out a "looking" verb using only their face and body — no words! Their partner guesses which verb it is. Then swap. Children can draw a verb card to choose their word, or pick their own favourite from the book.

Materials
  • Verb word cards with simple illustrations (one per pair)
  • Going Fishing with Nana for reference
peeping 🐸
peeking 🦘
Curriculum Links

📋 VEYLDF Learning Outcome 5

Children's developing understanding of how word choice shapes meaning, and their growing ability to use language to describe and interpret the world around them.

Vocabulary Depth Oral Language Embodied Learning
Diverse Learners 🌏

Because the activity is grounded in physical movement and facial expression, children do not need strong oral language skills to participate meaningfully — making it accessible to EAL/D learners and children with language delays. The paired "You Do, I Guess" format gives every child the opportunity to both express and interpret meaning at their own level. Visual word cards support children who benefit from multimodal input. For more advanced learners, they can be challenged to use the verb in a full sentence when guessing.

📚 Academic Justification

Nation et al. (2022) highlighted that picture books expose children to vocabulary that is qualitatively richer than everyday speech, providing valuable opportunities to encounter semantically related words in meaningful contexts. The nine "looking" verbs in this book form exactly this kind of semantically related word group, where each term shares a broad meaning but differs in emotional tone and intensity. Read and Quirke (2018) further found that the placement of new verbs within a rhyming storybook context supports children's ability to map word meanings. The drama-based approach and paired guessing game extend this learning by inviting children to embody each verb physically and use it communicatively, connecting word meaning to lived experience and supporting deeper vocabulary understanding.

If the video below does not load, click here to watch: https://youtu.be/y9dWgiOrIbo

🎬 Activity 2 Demo Video
Ways of Looking
🎯
Activity 3 · Descriptive Language

Animal Adjective Matching Game

Overview

Children are given picture cards of the animals from the book and a set of adjective word cards. They match each animal to its adjective from the story, then extend by thinking of their own descriptive words.

lazy 🦘
noisy 🦜
Steps
  1. Lay out the animal picture cards face up.
  2. Introduce each adjective card — say the word, act it out, ask "Which animal does this describe?"
  3. Children match adjective cards to animal pictures.
  4. Extension: "Can you think of another word to describe this animal?"
  5. Create a class "adjective wall" displaying the matched pairs.
Materials
  • Animal picture cards (frog, jabiru, snake, crocodile, kangaroo, dragonfly, ant, galah)
  • Adjective word cards (slimy, graceful, slithering, cunning, lazy, fluttering, hungry, noisy)
  • Large display space for the adjective wall
Curriculum Links

📋 VEYLDF Learning Outcome 5

Supporting children's understanding of describing words. Also connects to the cross-curriculum priority of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Histories and Cultures — all featured animals are native to the Australian landscape.

Descriptive Language First Nations Context Visual Literacy
Diverse Learners 🌏

The picture-based matching format allows children with limited English proficiency to engage through visual recognition before they can produce vocabulary orally. The extension task provides challenge for children ready to move beyond the text. For First Nations students, the inclusion of animals from their Country may provide a meaningful cultural connection and an opportunity to share knowledge from their own community.

📚 Academic Justification

Montag et al. (2015) found that picture books contain a greater variety of unique word types than everyday parent speech, making shared reading a valuable opportunity for children to encounter vocabulary unlikely to appear in daily conversation. The adjectives in this book are particularly well-suited to explicit teaching because they are paired with vivid illustrations that provide visual support for meaning-making. When children then generate their own adjectives in the extension task, they move from receptive to expressive vocabulary use, reflecting a deepening of word knowledge. Dowdall et al. (2020) further noted that the benefits of shared picture book reading are strengthened through repeated engagement with the same text, suggesting that revisiting this book across multiple sessions will continue to support children's descriptive language development.

Academic Sources

References 📖

  • Adam, H., & Barratt-Pugh, C. (2020). The challenge of monoculturalism: What books are educators sharing with children and what messages do they send? The Australian Educational Researcher, 47(5), 815–836. https://doi.org/10.1007/s13384-019-00375-7
  • Dowdall, N., Melendez-Torres, G. J., Murray, L., Gardner, F., Hartford, L., & Cooper, P. J. (2020). Shared picture book reading interventions for child language development: A systematic review and meta-analysis. Child Development, 91(2), e383–e399. https://doi.org/10.1111/cdev.13225
  • Justice, L. M., & Ezell, H. K. (2002). Use of storybook reading to increase print awareness in at-risk children. American Journal of Speech-Language Pathology, 11(1), 17–29. https://doi.org/10.1044/1058-0360(2002/003)
  • Montag, J. L., Jones, M. N., & Smith, L. B. (2015). The words children hear: Picture books and the statistics for language learning. Psychological Science, 26(9), 1489–1496. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797615594361
  • Nation, K., Dawson, N. J., & Hsiao, Y. (2022). Book language and its implications for children's language, literacy, and development. Current Directions in Psychological Science, 31(4), 368–375. https://doi.org/10.1177/09637214221103264
  • Read, K., & Quirke, J. (2018). Rhyme and word placement in storybooks support high-level verb mapping in 3- to 5-year-olds. Frontiers in Psychology, 9, Article 889. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2018.00889
  • Victorian Early Years Learning and Development Framework (VEYLDF). https://www.vic.gov.au/victorian-early-years-learning-development-framework-veyldf
  • Ziolkowski, R. A., & Goldstein, H. (2008). Effects of an embedded phonological awareness intervention during repeated book reading on preschool children with language delays. Journal of Early Intervention, 31(1), 67–90. https://doi.org/10.1177/1053815108324808
